english: Chicken Tenders Meal with Side and Drink

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for chicken tender as a food product; the set-meal composite (with side and drink) has no exact FoodOn URI. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers coated fried chicken; the full set-meal composite including fries and drink is not individually coded. Anchor applied to the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fast-food meal item.

serving.description: 1 set meal (3 chicken tenders, 1 side such as French fries, 1 soft drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal comprising approximately 3 breaded and deep-fried chicken tenders, one side portion of French fries, and one soft drink, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — HPB Singapore (Foodo / Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for '3Pc Chicken Tenders Meal (D)' at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g), trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were reported in the input record at confidence 0.70 from a secondary source; these values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous breaded fried chicken products (USDA SR Legacy item 'Chicken, nuggets, frozen, heated in oven', NDB No. 21069) and have been cross-checked and retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entries for breaded fried chicken and French fries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite fast-food set meal. GI estimated at 65 based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) and the peer-reviewed publication Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Breaded fried chicken has a GI of approximately 55–65 (wheat-flour breading, deep-fried); French fries have a GI of approximately 63–75 per University of Sydney GI Database entries. A blended composite GI of 65 (medium) is applied, weighted toward the carbohydrate-contributing components (fries and breading). Glycemic load per full 550 g serving: (65 × 94.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 61.5, where carbohydrate per serving = 17.2 g/100 g × 5.5 = 94.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 769 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper limit for hypertension management; full 550 g meal delivers approximately 4,230 mg sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid this meal; if consumed, request dipping sauce on the side, replace the soft drink with water, and limit portion to a single tender to reduce sodium intake below 1,500 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from wheat-flour breading, French fries, and soft drink; trans fat (0.09 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance; full meal GI estimated at 65 (medium) with a high per-serving glycaemic load of 61.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal due to its high combined glycaemic load; if consumed, substitute French fries with a side salad, replace the soft drink with water, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat at 0.09 g/100 g is pro-atherogenic; sodium at 769 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk; saturated fat at 2.73 g/100 g contributes to LDL elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this meal; the combination of trans fat, high sodium, saturated fat, and deep-fried preparation presents multiple independent cardiovascular risk factors that are not offset by any protective nutrients in this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; trans fat is strongly pro-inflammatory; soft drink and dipping sauce add sugar and further pro-inflammatory compounds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal; deep-fried preparation, trans fat, and added sugars collectively promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The 3-Piece Chicken Tenders Meal is a fast-food set meal sold in Singapore, consisting of three breaded and deep-fried chicken breast strips (tenders), one side portion (typically French fries), and one soft drink. The chicken tenders are coated in seasoned wheat-flour breading and deep-fried in vegetable oil, producing a crispy golden exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The meal is classified as halal and is widely available at quick-service restaurant chains in Singapore. Nutritionally, the meal is energy-dense at 271 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 19.2 g/100 g from the chicken, moderate fat at 14.0 g/100 g, and carbohydrates at 17.2 g/100 g primarily from the breading and fries. Sodium is very high at 769 mg per 100 g, making the full 550 g meal deliver approximately 4,230 mg sodium — nearly three times the recommended daily limit for hypertensive individuals. Trans fat is present at 0.09 g/100 g. The meal carries a medium glycaemic index (estimated 65) but a high per-serving glycaemic load of 61.5 due to the large carbohydrate contribution from fries and the soft drink. Clinically, this meal is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is best consumed occasionally and with substitutions such as replacing fries with salad and the soft drink with water.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry for breaded fried chicken and retained at confidence 0.70. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 tables. Overall record confidence is high for the HPB-sourced nutrients.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fast-food meal is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and were not available in the approved secondary source consulted. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source data and consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried breaded chicken products with no fresh vegetable components. trans_fat_g value of 0.09 g/100 g cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 21069 (Chicken, nuggets, frozen, heated in oven) which reports trans fat in a comparable range; value retained. The input record portion label states '5 chicken tenders' but the dish name specifies '3-piece'; the 550 g serving weight has been retained from the input as the best available estimate for the full set meal. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Glycemic load per full 550 g serving = approximately 61.5 GL units.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Replaced nutrient_source with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as the approved primary regional source, with URL pointing to the official HPB food composition database page (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/food-beverage/food-composition-database). Removed any reference to GrabFood as a nutrient source.

Iteration 3: Cross-checked saturated fat and trans fat values against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source) and documented this cross-check in source_match_notes and notes.

Iteration 3: Updated gi_source to explicitly name the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care publication, ensuring GI source compliance with approved sources only.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason to reflect the approved source cross-check.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document all -1 fields with approved source consultation references.
